reasoning: The flagged scripts (ppls/pplsyu) are user-facing tools installed by the package, not executed during makepkg. Their functionality falls within expected package manager operations. Qwen's concerns about input sanitization and pacman bypass are valid design considerations but don't constitute supply-chain risks during package build/install.
disagreement_analysis: Qwen misinterpreted user-facing scripts as build-time threats. Deepseek correctly focused on makepkg execution scope.
false_positives: ppls: Flagged for unsanitized input but runs post-installation, not during makepkg
pplsyu: Bypass claim ignores pacman's signature verification still occurs during installation
learnings: Improve scope awareness: Models should better distinguish build-time vs user-time scripts
Enhance prompt clarity: Explicitly state that user-executed tools are out-of-scope unless triggered by PKGBUILD
Address false positive pattern: Reduce over-alerting on package manager wrapper scripts
